review_text: This is one of the very few games where the visuals put it over the top for me. I don't love the combat or traversal, and the writing is fine, but Night City is just so good-looking. In fact, I started the game on a less powerful rig and just couldn't get into it; I got a slightly better laptop that runs Cyberpunk on high settings with ray tracing, and now I'm hooked. Still, for such a visual game, the character animations can be pretty cringy.

review_text: Bought this game wayyyy back when it came out on December 10th, 2020, but never touch the game after it was a buggy mess and full of glitches which was unplayable and totally attrocious. After the game recieved many negative reviews, CDPR took action and tried their best to fix the problems. Thus, 1.5/1.6 update came out and I started playing the game around 2023. A lot has been fixed, just minor bugs and glitches here and there, but it did not affect my gameplay whatsoever. Now coming to the storyline and graphics, no words I can describe how amazing, beautiful and gorgeous the graphic is. Playing on my old PC at medium to high graphics the game still look pretty and aesthetic as always. Now that I have upgraded my PC, I was able to run extreme max out cyberpsycho graphics settings with path tracing enabled (no my PC didn't explode) and wow I managed to capture in game screenshots that looked super realistic like it was real life. I was totally astounded by how gameplay was very smooth and no crashes compared to their buggy release. Then now with the storyline, you play as V a mercenary that have the options to choose three life paths. My first play through was female V Corpo which was pretty interesting, because each life paths there would be an introduction to meet or get to know your best friend Jackie and do missions together. There will be unique dialogues for each life paths when having conservation with people all over NC and there will be certain consequences that you will love or regret choosing. All I can say the game is full of action packed and the best part is that you can chrome up your cyberware which is pretty badass! You can also become a full netrunner build or full David Martinez build. With update 2.0 released a few years ago which was Phantom Liberty update, all I can say that CDPR totally redeemed themselves in becoming one of the best game to ever make. The game felt much alive and less bugs or glitches can be seen throughout the gameplay. I do sure hope that Cyberpunk 2 or Cyberpunk Orion will be absolutely mind blowing. Hopefully to see V in the future, maybe as a fixer or once a badass Night CIty legend that roam the streets and now ventured across the world or the moon. Once again, GOOOOOOOD MORNING NIGHT CITY! Get out there and have fun chooms!

review_text: My, my, my—this game is truly stunning. Visually, it’s absolutely beautiful, and the storyline is far more emotionally impactful than you might expect. It never really gets boring, and I found myself consistently engaged; in fact, scratch that I am STILL engaged. Story-wise, this is easily one of the best—if not the best—games I’ve ever played. I highly recommend it, especially if you enjoy narrative-driven games. Overall, the game feels like playing a more sophisticated version of GTA, with deeper storytelling and a more immersive world. That said, the police system isn’t nearly as advanced, which makes it pretty darn easy to escape during chases. Of course, this isn’t meant to be GTA, so it’s not a dealbreaker, but nonetheless, it would make the game feel more polished. On the technical side, I’m running the game on a Mac with an M2 chip. It performs smoothly at around 30 frames per second, which makes it very playable. The only issue I’ve encountered is with mouse and keyboard input—there’s some input lag, which makes it less smooth and honestly not very enjoyable without a controller. Nice thing is though, with a controller comes haptic feedback ;)

review_text: OK. Cyberpunk 2077 was a tough one for me. I started this game years ago, when it was still a buggy mess. I couldn't get into it. And not only because of the bugs. I couldn't get into the story. There was too much happening, too many characters introduced, too much cyber-lingo I didn't understand, until I was phasing out during the conversations. I gave up after the first 15-20 hours. Came back to the game after Phantom Liberty, and decided to start a new character. It was a bit better, less bugs, and I had a better grasp of the world, story and characters. But it was still a bit much. I got lost with how much the game was throwing at me. I forgot who each person was, Got calls or messages from people I was supposed to know, but didn't remember, and couldn't give a s*** about. And I realized I wasn't enjoying the most important part of the experience: the world, the characters, the story. I was enjoying the combat, but going through the game just checking every icon on the map, not caring about anything or anyone. I stopped, and uninstalled. After two years without thinking about it, I stumbled upon the great Edgerunners show. And it pulled me back into this world. I decided to do things right this time. Installed the game, and modded it to improve the experience as much as possible with the "Welcome to Night City" mod list. Started a new game, and went in with mi mind set on really paying attention to the stories, not just completing tasks. And the game finally clicked for me. This was a hard game to get into, specially since I am more of a casual player nowadays, being a dad and having two jobs. It took me 9 to 10 months to finish it, because I can't just do the main missions. I like seeing as much as possible, completing every fixer gig and side mission. The only things I didn't fully complete were the crimes, assaults, criminal activities and races. Well, after so many hours, I was on the final stretch, and I couldn't stop. Stayed awake all night. I needed to see how it ended. And I am so glad I did it. It was rewarding, exhausting (in a good way). There are so many little things that make the world so authentic. Little call backs to previous events, character I crossed paths with that show up, and recall what happened previously. It's fair to say, Doing all those missions was worth it. I really enjoyed some of the side content, and some deep, strong and uncomfortable topics the game touches on. I liked the moral ambiguities that are depicted. (Almost) nothing is ever black or white, and you're faced with some nerve racking decisions to make, many times not fully knowing the consequences of your actions. These are the things that stayed with me the most. The ending was bitter sweet, but so fitting to this world. And I'm happy with the one I got. V's story ended on a sad but hopeful note, and open enough to interpretation that leaves room for me to imagine what comes next. Also, the people I cared about (mostly) got their stories wrapped up in a satisfying way, and I'm so happy I got to know them. Although I'm NOT PLAYING THIS GAME EVER AGAIN, or any other open world RPG in the near future, I couldn't be happier of having decided to come back and giving Cyberpunk this last chance. It finally did it for me. Can I recommend this game? YES. ABSOLUTELY YES. Specially with a few mods to improve the experience. It's not for everyone. It's very hard to get into, it demands too much of your time and energy, it has too many characters, too many stories, and they don't always work in sync with one another. It can get messy and confusing. The gameplay systems can also be a bit overwhelming, specially if you're not familiar with RPG mechanics. But Night City is a place I'll never forget, and its people and stories will be in my mind for many, many years.

review_text: I struggle to see games to the end. I've had Elden Ring since its release, and for the first four years, I've done several playthroughs, all unfinished. Still, I saw Elden Ring in high regard, even though I could not bring myself to finish it. This year, I've set for myself the goal to commit to the games I start, to a varying level of success. There are already a few games which I dropped early, but I've also finished a few. After finishing another game, I decided to start an Elden Ring playthrough, without really thinking I would finally complete the game. WIthout any expectations, I ended up clearing both the base game and DLC. Now that I have completed it, I feel somewhat empty, not even sure what game to follow it up with. Elden Ring is a great game, one of the best games of our modern times. 10/10

review_text: A masterpiece. Playing on Xbox One at launch was rough due to performance issues (the skulls with runes couldn’t even spawn!). Now, years later and playing on PC, I can engage with this game fully. And by doing so I have come to the conclusion that I prefer ‘Lies of P’. The parry mechanic in that game felt most rewarding, and the ability to retrieve your souls OUTside of a boss arena was appreciated. Should be a default mechanic. The combat, too, was less clunky. I sit here now having just fought a dragonkin boss in Elden Ring and realised that enemies large in scale make for poor melee combat, with me battling the lock-on system more than the enemy itself. Loses half a star because, frankly, some of the questlines are ridiculous without a guide. I love an ‘information collective’ (Henry Jenkins) and the cryptic storyline is wonderful, but what do you mean I have to reload Fia three times over to experience the end of her questline. That’s just silly, and not obtuse in a fun way. Genuinely detracts from the overall experience and has meant that I’m rigorously following a step-by-step guide to experience everything in the game rather than just letting loose and exploring for my own sake. Is that a me problem? Yes. But this is my review.

review_text: (On playstation: Finished Demon's Souls, Dark Souls 1/2/3. Platinumed Bloodborne and Elden Ring) Maybe not the hardest Soulslike (probably Dark Souls 3), but the most challenging. And, even though it's open world, it's still the most approachable Soulslike I've played. It has the best story, the best art, and the best character build customization. It is a requirement to build your character wisely but this is still a fair, skill-based game that never grows tedious and is endlessly rewarding. I thought Bloodborne was the pinnacle until I finished this.

review_text: Before this game, I hadn't played a turn-based RPG like this, but I'm glad I did. I've never experienced a story and narrative as deep and thoughtfully executed as in Baldur's Gate 3. The visuals are great, too. There are so many playstyles and builds to experiment with until you find what fits best for you. It's a lot of fun. Exploration pays off well, rewarding you with hidden areas, loot, and even side quest opportunities. The choice and karma system runs deep. Your decisions matter and directly influence your party and camp. I rate this game a 10/10 and highly recommend it!

review_text: This is a highly rated game for a reason, it is a top down table top RPG where you control a band of adventurers in a very D&D like game. There are some things I dont like:- 1. it a huge install (over 100Gb seems unnecessary) probably due to the large amount of cut scenes 2. Its a massive game, I feel I have barely scratched the surface 3. its tres difficult (not souls difficult, but close) expect to repeat some of the boss fight scenes dozens of times and use loads of scum saves. This is too much for me, boo hoo. 4. Wish I could get to a proper 3rd person view with the camera. There might be a mod for this, but I have yet to find it. Ideally I would add a switchable first person view (or even VR) Despite the above I highly recommend this game, especially if you are into the whole D&D/lord of the rings type of universe. This game really is fantastically produced and puts the Bethesda to shame in some ways. Quality.

title: The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt
game_description: You are Geralt of Rivia, mercenary monster slayer. Before you stands a war-torn, monster-infested continent you can explore at will. Your current contract? Tracking down Ciri — the Child of Prophecy, a living weapon that can alter the shape of the world.
review_text: This is an amazing game dragged down by technical issues. The "enhanced" version can look pretty good but not good enough forgive the abysmal performance and constant crashing. On a 4070TI with DLSS set to "Balanced" on max settings the framerate will randomly go down to single digits in major cities. It's unplayable in those instances. This game should not run dramatically worse than games that are far more graphically complex with full path tracing. And then you have the crashing. In game it's pretty infrequent but when continuing/loading a save, I kid you not, it will crash to desktop 50% of the time. Sometimes multiple times in a row. It's such a bummer to see one of my favorite games of all time in this state.

title: The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt
game_description: You are Geralt of Rivia, mercenary monster slayer. Before you stands a war-torn, monster-infested continent you can explore at will. Your current contract? Tracking down Ciri — the Child of Prophecy, a living weapon that can alter the shape of the world.
review_text: As a fan of [i]The Witcher 2[/i], I knew I had to see how my choices played out in [b]The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt[/b]. It delivers an even grittier, more cinematic experience—this time with the massive, seamless open world that truly feels like a consequence of the wars I witnessed in the previous game. The story, following Geralt's search for Ciri, is gripping and deeply personal, but it’s the ghosts of my past decisions that make it feel unique to me. That said, the save file import was the major talking point for me. I was initially skeptical about how much my specific path would be recognized—until I realized how much it reminded me of the branching consequences in [i]Detroit: Become Human[/i]. Having walked Vernon Roche’s path in [i]Assassins of Kings[/i], meeting him again in the backstreets of Novigrad felt like catching up with an old brother-in-arms. Because I chose to save Triss at Loc Muinne, the political landscape for mages felt earned, and my decision to forgive Letho led to one of the most rewarding "full circle" moments in the game. Seeing a character I could have easily killed in the last game actually standing before me, offering his help, added a layer of moral weight I haven't felt since my time with the most reactive RPGs. One thing is for sure: I am finally ready to jump into [b]Cyberpunk 2077[/b] after this. The world-building CD Projekt Red achieved here is too rich to ignore, and I’m dying to see if they can replicate this level of narrative continuity in a new setting. My only hope is that the "choices matter" DNA remains intact. After seeing how they respected my loyalty to Roche and my mercy toward Letho, I want to see them push boundaries even further in a futuristic setting without losing that grounded, character-driven soul. Gameplay-wise, the acting by Doug Cockle and the return of familiar faces is top-tier and sets a high bar. The combat feels like a fluid, high-budget evolution of the second game’s mechanics—the signs feel more integrated, and the alchemy system is more accessible without losing its depth. Some of the map markers in Skellige can feel a bit overwhelming compared to the focused hubs of [i]Witcher 2[/i], and the weight of the political jargon is still heavy, but the payoff of seeing your personal history reflected in the world is worth it. TL;DR 🎮 [i]Witcher 2[/i] veterans will love seeing Roche and Letho return based on their choices. 🧠 A masterpiece of storytelling where "mercy" or "loyalty" from the past actually pays off. 🌍 The transition to a true open world makes the Northern Kingdoms feel massive yet personal. ⏳ Cyberpunk is next! If the consequences are even half as deep as this, I’m sold. Rating: ★★★★★
summary: As a fan of [i]The Witcher 2[/i], I knew I had to see how my choices played out in [b]The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt[/b]. It delivers an even grittier, more cinematic experience—this time with the massive, seamless open world that truly feels like a consequence of the wars I witnessed in the previous game. The story, following Geralt's search for Ciri, is gripping and deeply personal, but it’s the ghosts of my past decisions that make it feel unique to me. That said, the save file import was the major talking point for me. I was initially skeptical about how much my specific path would be recognized—until I realized how much it reminded me of the branching consequences in [i]Detroit: Become Human[/i]. Having walked Vernon Roche’s path in [i]Assassins of Kings[/i], meeting him again in the backstreets of Novigrad felt like catching up with an old brother-in-arms. Because I chose to save Triss at Loc Muinne, the political landscape for mages felt earned, and my decision to forgive Letho led to one of the most rewarding "full circle" moments in the game. Seeing a character I could have easily killed in the last game actually standing before me, offering his help, added a layer of moral weight I haven't felt since my time with the most reactive RPGs. One thing is for sure: I am finally ready to jump into [b]Cyberpunk 2077[/b] after this. The world-building CD Projekt Red achieved here is too rich to ignore, and I’m dying to see if they can replicate this level of narrative continuity in a new setting. My only hope is that the "choices matter" DNA remains intact. After seeing how they respected my loyalty to Roche and my mercy toward Letho, I want to see them push boundaries even further in a futuristic setting without losing that grounded, character-driven soul. Gameplay-wise, the acting by Doug Cockle and the return of familiar faces is top-tier and sets a high bar. The combat feels like a fluid, high-budget evolution of the second game’s mechanics—the signs feel more integrated, and the alchemy system is more accessible without losing its depth. Some of the map markers in Skellige can feel a bit overwhelming compared to the focused hubs of [i]Witcher 2[/i], and the weight of the political jargon is still heavy, but the payoff of seeing your personal history reflected in the world is worth it. If the consequences are even half as deep as this, I’m sold. Rating: ★★★★★
